passage:
A sleep researcher tested the average response time (in milliseconds) of participants on a cognitive reaction test after different amounts of sleep the previous night.

| Hours of Sleep | Average Response Time (ms) |
|---|---|
| 4 hours | 412 |
| 5 hours | 368 |
| 6 hours | 321 |
| 7 hours | 289 |
| 8 hours | 275 |
| 9 hours | 278 |

The researcher concluded that reaction time improves (decreases) as sleep increases up to eight hours, with little additional benefit beyond that.
question: Which finding from the data most directly supports the researcher's conclusion?

choices.A: Participants who slept 4 hours had the slowest average response time at 412 ms.
choices.B: Response time decreases consistently from 4 to 8 hours of sleep (412 ms to 275 ms), but the difference between 8 and 9 hours of sleep is only 3 ms—negligible compared to the 137 ms improvement seen between 4 and 8 hours.
choices.C: Participants who slept 9 hours had a slightly slower response time (278 ms) than those who slept 8 hours (275 ms).
choices.D: The largest single-hour improvement occurs between 6 and 7 hours of sleep.

explanation: The researcher's conclusion has two components: (1) response time improves as sleep increases up to 8 hours, and (2) there is little additional benefit beyond 8 hours. Choice B addresses both components directly: it notes the consistent improvement from 4 to 8 hours (412 to 275 ms, a 137 ms improvement) and then highlights that the difference between 8 and 9 hours is negligible (only 3 ms). This two-part evidence directly matches the two-part conclusion. Choice A addresses only one end of the sleep range. Choice C notes only the 8-to-9-hour comparison without establishing the broader improvement trend. Choice D identifies a specific single-hour improvement without addressing the plateau after 8 hours.
